excerpt_en: Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about the theme "Grooming Matters." ▼Scary, but... So, today is Saturday! No classes. I didn't have any plans to hang out with anyone today, so
body_en:
Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about the theme

"Grooming Matters."

▼Scary, but...

So, today is Saturday!

No classes.

I didn't have any plans to

hang out with anyone today,

so I decided to go to a hair salon.

I could have gone to the barber shop near school, but a friend of mine went there a month ago and it turned out kind of mediocre, so I headed to the hair salon at IT Park (a shopping mall).

About 2-3 weeks ago, I had gone to that salon for a friend's makeover, so I already knew the vibe and the location was no problem.

It was my first time ever getting a haircut outside of Japan.

*My haircut virginity was taken in Cebu.

Honestly, I was thinking, "What if it turns out all choppy..."

But thanks to showing them a photo

and explaining in English,

it didn't turn out too crazy.

It had already been over

2 months since my last haircut,

and my sideburns were getting messy,

so it was good to get that problem solved.

I also added an eyebrow trim as an option

and got them shaped up.

The total was 550 pesos - about 1,500 yen in Japanese currency.

So cheap, as expected!!

What was different from Japanese salons:

the towel they wrap around you at the start was "toilet paper,"

and the post-haircut shower was cold water (no hot water).

Other than that, nothing was particularly different.

I've traveled overseas many times for work and sightseeing, but this is my first time staying abroad for more than a month, and it really hit me again that I'm "living overseas."

After that, I went for a massage

to refresh my body!

This time it was 60 minutes.

Then,

I was just going to take a taxi home,

but since I was out on my own

and it was a 40-minute walk to school,

it wasn't exactly marathon training, but

I decided to walk.

*I couldn't do this if I were with someone else.

Listening to Voicy while walking,

40 minutes flies by.

I didn't make any detours,

but it was pretty enjoyable.

After getting back to school,

I changed clothes right away

and headed to the rooftop gym.

I ran for about 40 minutes.

With the 42km Medoc Marathon already set,

and the fact that the people around me who are living life to the fullest

all somehow love running,

have great stamina, and are healthy,

I figured I should follow their lead,

so I've been running 5-6 times a week.

*5km to 8km

For me, running

isn't something I love,

but it's about building stamina,

and since I've been eating more over here,

I'm also aware that I need to maintain my body properly!

This is also part of grooming.

I used to maintain a stylish figure without doing anything thanks to being "young," but with my 30s approaching and the feeling that things would go south if I kept on like this, I'm glad I was able to make running a habit while here in Cebu.

So today, I wandered around Cebu,

did some stamina-building training after coming back,

and then worked on FOMUS-related business,

and before I knew it, it was 10 PM.

I don't have any particular plans for tomorrow either,

but I think I'll go out somewhere and find a place where I can have conversations with locals!

excerpt_en: Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about the theme "Connecting People." ▼Being taken to worlds I don't know So, today is Sunday. I went to a cafe and to check out vintage clothes with friends.
body_en:
Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about the theme

"Connecting People."

▼Being taken to worlds I don't know

So, today is Sunday.

I went to a cafe and to check out vintage clothes with friends.

I'm not personally interested in vintage clothes,

but I was interested in local events, so I decided to go.

The start time was 3 PM,

so we arrived a little early, but as expected of the Philippines -

hardly anyone was there at 3.

*Both staff and customers.

People started gathering around 3:30.

Since it was a flea market, clothes and bags from various countries were on display. I had zero interest in the products, but

it was fun chatting with the vendors in English.

It's a place I would never go to on my own,

but because I was invited, I got to see this scenery, and that was interesting.

Even if you're not interested,

there's so much to learn,

so I was reminded once again that I should treasure opportunities like these.

Then, in the evening,

I had a meeting with a team I'm consulting for back in Japan.

Even though I'm in Cebu, we live in an era where you can work online, so I make full use of what's available.

After the meeting,

I chatted with the Korean students on the school rooftop.

Since there were a lot of Koreans,

they'd periodically switch to Korean,

which I couldn't understand at all,

but if I said "ENGLISH!" they'd switch back to English. Haha.

When people from the same country gather,

they tend to slip into their native language,

so you have to be careful about that!

▼Hosting an event in Cebu in April!

Now, changing the subject,

I've decided to host an event

in Cebu in April.

I'll be inviting the models

I personally met and did Masu Photo shoots with.

I'd been thinking about this since February -

I wanted to have one more chance

to spend time communicating with the models

right before I head home.

One of FOMUS's missions is

"connecting people through masu."

Once you've done a FOMUS Masu Photo, you're part of the crew!

Right now, it's only me and each model who are connected.

What I want to do is bring together people who share

the common experience and memory of "having done a Masu Photo shoot,"

and make them feel that

"I'm glad I got involved with FOMUS and MaSU."

Especially since horizontal connections

are hard to build once you're an adult.

That's because there are

so few places for those kinds of encounters.

However, FOMUS has the unique format of

"photography, traditional Japanese masu, community, and art,"

and the advantage that

I can host an event all by myself.

This is possible precisely because I've spent 5 years

hosting events on my own.

Nothing I've done has been wasted.

On top of that, the idea that a Japanese person with a Japanese brand is connecting people in Cebu is pretty cool, and if something new comes out of these connections, that would be amazing.

"Being connected because you hold a FOMUS masu"

is something that

AI absolutely cannot do.

This is something only possible because I've been

pounding the pavement, meeting people one by one.

It costs time and money (transportation, cafes, etc.),

but I'm building connections that are worth far more than that.

So today, I sent event invitations

to the models I've shot with so far.

The message went something like this...

How are you? Thank you for your cooperation as a model for the shoot the other day!

I will be leaving Cebu on April 26th and heading back to Japan.

During this time, I'd like to say hello again to those who participated as Masu Photo models, and I'd like to connect everyone who participated in the shoots.

So I'm planning to host a lunch gathering on April 21st (Sunday)!

*Around 11 AM to 2 PM.

If you're able to attend, please let me know by April 6th!

Once the number of attendees is confirmed, I'll decide on the venue.

*It will likely be near Ayala Mall or IT Park.

I'm looking forward to seeing you again!

I sent a message like this in English.

And already, 6 people have said

they'd love to attend.

Some others said they'd adjust their schedules,

and I thought - even though it hasn't happened yet - how lucky I am that they'd want to meet again.

This is one step forward toward

my goal of "connecting people around the world through FOMUS masu."

And beyond that,

when people from Cebu come to Japan,

I'll introduce and connect them with my friends in Japan.

And vice versa.

My role is

to strengthen the FOMUS brand

and to establish masu as a communication tool.

And to connect people with people.

As I wrote earlier,

"I'm glad I have a FOMUS masu!"

Increasing the number of people who feel this way

is what makes the FOMUS brand even stronger.

I'll make sure to do what can only be done

offline (in real life)!!

So yeah,

I'm already super excited about this!

I still need to find a venue though. Haha.

title_en: Becoming Someone People Want to Work With
excerpt_en: Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about the theme "Becoming Someone People Want to Work With." ▼Something that's always been inside me My activities in Cebu have entered their 8th week. I'm continuing to study English seriously,
body_en:
Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about the theme

"Becoming Someone People Want to Work With."

▼Something that's always been inside me

My activities in Cebu have entered their 8th week.

I'm continuing to study English seriously.

That said, outputting what I've learned here

isn't really the point, so I'll skip that.

I want to talk about myself.

The masu brand "FOMUS"

was launched in February 2021,

and I'm still tenaciously keeping it going.

The reason I can continue these activities

is because all of you purchase these activity journals, and I'm truly grateful.

I'm incredibly supported by the people around me.

FOMUS is the ultimate example of that.

Because even though

I founded the FOMUS brand,

I couldn't have accomplished anything on my own.

It's because I have friends and allies around me

that I've been able to get through tough times.

And FOMUS's services and content

are created by teaming up with many specialists on projects,

generating new value.

For example,

FOMUS's character "KUKU"

was created by teaming up with professional designer K-san.

I used AI to create images

of the character I wanted to make,

then wrote detailed instruction documents to go with them,

and 6 characters wearing masu around their necks - something AI can't create - were born.

Furthermore, to bring KUKU to the world,

starting in February of this year, I teamed up with F-san, a video editor who works on TV productions, to launch the "KUKU x Music" project.

Basically, FOMUS projects

are usually driven by 2 or 3 people - me and someone else.

That's because 2-3 people is the ideal size for smooth communication.

Through my life as an address hopper,

meeting many people in person

and communicating with them,

multiple projects are running in parallel.

And today,

a new project kicked off.

It's apparel.

It started because I decided

to run the Medoc Marathon in France.

From there, I pulled in two Japanese people,

and now 3 of us from FOMUS will be participating.

One of the charms of this event

is running in costumes,

and the Medoc Marathon attracts wine-loving runners and media from around the world, making it a perfect promotional opportunity.

So I figured, why not

wear something super cool and eye-catching

while running the Medoc Marathon,

have fun, and promote FOMUS at the same time?

In other words, I want to run a marathon

with masu attached to my body!

*Crazy, right?

So I used AI to create rough sketches.

These are just concepts.

I posted these on

Instagram Stories asking,

"Can anyone make these clothes for me?"

but the response was zero.

Not surprising - it's hard to find someone who can make clothes with masu attached for running.

But I know two

professional clothing designers.

One of them specializes in making marathon wear.

They're a very popular designer,

and when I reached out on a long shot,

they said,

"Sounds fun, I'm in."

They actually said yes!

This designer is actually

the Taiwanese designer who designed our wear

for the Taiwan cycling project back in May/June of last year.

He's also a runner.

During the Taiwan cycling project, I wasn't running but cycling and doing photography,

but I had many great conversations with him,

and after the Taiwan project ended,

he even came to a FOMUS event

we held in Japan, and we toasted with masu together.

I can only be grateful to GPS Runner Shimizu-san

for creating this connection.

As a result,

he agreed to do the clothing design!!

The fact that so many people

are willing to collaborate on

my and FOMUS's projects

fills me with genuine happiness.

However,

just being happy about it isn't enough.

I think it's incredibly important to understand why people are willing to participate in these projects.

After graduating and working as a company employee,

there was always something on my mind.

That is,

"I want to work with MaSU!"

"I want to do something fun with MaSU!"

I want to be that kind of person.

In other words, by being involved with me,

people could go to places they've never seen before,

or feel like they want to be part of this once-in-a-lifetime journey -

those thoughts have always been in the back of my mind.

As a result,

I launched the masu brand FOMUS

and moved projects forward

under the umbrella of Japanese traditional craftsmanship.

"If MaSU's doing something fun, I'm in"

- hearing that is honestly what makes me happiest.

Of course, not every project succeeds.

Still, having people willing to be involved is something I'm deeply grateful for.

I still have a long way to go,

but the fact that being involved with MaSU has had a positive impact on people is because this has always been on my mind.

After all, if you don't have it in your mind,

it's hard to put it into action.

Today too, I was messaging with a model

I shot with in February,

and about the event I'm hosting in April -

she said she'll definitely attend!

She doesn't even know what kind of event it'll be (there aren't even preview photos), yet she committed to attending.

I think this is largely due to the impact of FOMUS's Masu Photo

and the fact that I've been communicating with each person individually.

With the people who are involved

expanding not just in Japan but around the world,

I want to make FOMUS even stronger

and create a world where everyone can proudly show it off.

And no matter which country I go to,

there will be people holding FOMUS masu,

and I want to build a reality where people can easily connect.

To aim for a world

where everyone can happily

raise a toast with FOMUS masu,

I'll keep polishing myself more and more,

and together with the greatest allies

who want to do something with MaSU,

I'll take FOMUS to the world.

*A photo from the Taiwan project.

title_en: A Story About Feeling a Little Lonely (Side Story)
excerpt_en: Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about the theme "A Story About Feeling a Little Lonely (Side Story)." ▼A sudden sad event I've been talking about really serious stuff lately, so today I'll take a breather.
body_en:
Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about the theme

"A Story About Feeling a Little Lonely (Side Story)."

▼A sudden sad event

I've been talking about really serious stuff lately,

so today I'll take a breather.

Feel free to skip this one entirely. Haha.

*I don't think you'll learn anything from reading this.

My language study in Cebu is now in its 8th week,

with 4 weeks (1 month) left.

As I wrote in a previous article,

I've already swapped out a bunch of teachers

and carefully selected the best people for my English learning.

*With thorough research, of course.

As a result,

I had gotten into a solid English-intensive groove,

but then something unexpected happened.

One of my teachers suddenly had to leave the school.

It was the teacher

I had discovered at a drinking party a few weeks back.

Honestly, her face was my type,

and I got her assigned as my teacher the following week,

but then she dropped out at this timing!

I know the reason.

The teacher didn't say anything specifically,

but she's expecting.

There's nothing I can do about that,

but not being able to take her classes anymore is sad.

"I want to be able to use English for business"

- separate from that strong desire,

"I want to be able to chat naturally with this teacher"

was a short-term goal I had set,

but that short-term goal vanished

at an unexpectedly early timing! Hahaha.

The problem with this is

that I have to choose a new teacher again.

Honestly, in my teacher database,

there are only about 2 more teachers

that I'd actually want to take classes from.

With this sudden announcement,

it felt like being turned down before even confessing! Haha.

Man, it's been a while since I've felt this way,

and I wanted to preserve this feeling, which is why I'm writing it down.

Beyond her looks,

her charm and the fun of our conversations

made me like her as a person, which is where these emotions come from.

I hadn't felt this way

in the past 3 years, so it's refreshing.

I wonder if this emotion emerged because

my base of activity shifted from Japan to overseas...

Also, the fact that we met

in a non-work context might be a big part of it.

Normally, when I meet models,

I'm in business mode and treat the shoots as work,

but this time, since we met in a context

completely unrelated to work,

I think that's why I became interested in her as a woman.

Being able to confirm that this ordinary emotion still exists within me was a small silver lining. Haha.

▼Making the most of this situation

I do feel disappointed,

but it's not like I got my heart broken,

so I need to choose a new teacher.

In my teacher database,

there were 2 teachers I'd enjoy having conversations with,

and one of them happened to have an opening in the slot I needed to fill, so I submitted a change request.

If I don't submit this,

I'll get assigned a teacher I have no interest in, so it's an important step.

*Nobody changes teachers as much as I do.

And as for the teacher

who'll be leaving soon,

it turns out that tomorrow afternoon during my free period,

she's also free,

so I asked her to do a Masu Photo shoot.

I did take some quick shots today,

but it was just so sudden... Haha.

I'm planning to have her wear

the masu accessories too!

*I really wanted a female model in her late 20s.

*Her looks are more than adequate.

Also, while the teacher is leaving the school,

she won't be hospitalized right away,

so tomorrow I'm going to negotiate

for her to be my English tutor

during the period I'm back in Japan.

*May and June.

Even if this negotiation fails,

I'll continue with online English lessons in Japan,

so if it works out, great;

if not, no real damage done.

Running the FOMUS brand has taught me to deal with all sorts of unexpected events and things not going as planned, and I've naturally developed the ability to respond calmly, so I'm glad my brain works this way now.

So yeah,

something slightly sad happened,

but I'm even enjoying that!

That's the story for today.

I rarely write about stuff like this, so this was a rare episode! Haha.

excerpt_en: Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about the theme "Taking One More Step Forward." ▼Second test So, it's the 8th week (2nd month), and at school, we had a test during the morning hours.
body_en:
Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about the theme

"Taking One More Step Forward."

▼Second test

So, it's the 8th week (2nd month),

and at school, we had a test during the morning hours.

It tests your speaking, listening, writing, and reading abilities.

Honestly, you can't expect your abilities to skyrocket in just 2 months,

and besides, I've been doing conversation-focused classes with my teachers,

so I haven't been studying reading or writing at all.

I've just been focused on wanting to be able to speak and wanting to understand what others are saying, and that's how I've been approaching my daily classes.

So I wasn't going to stress about test scores,

but I was hoping for at least 50 out of 80.

The results come out next week!

▼Making a move!

Now, here's the main topic for today.

As I wrote in yesterday's article,

my favorite teacher is leaving.

And today was the day to negotiate.

But of all things,

the test overlapped with class time,

so I didn't have a final class with that teacher. Haha.

But

I'm not the kind of guy who lets an opportunity slip like that.

Since I knew about this the day before,

I had asked her to keep the time after the test and after my afternoon classes open.

I also brought up doing a model shoot.

*She kept turning down the model request though. Haha.

After the test,

I headed straight to the teacher's room.

With this teacher, it never felt like we were having a class - we were just talking.

And the conversations were often about business,

so it was all upside for me.

She kept refusing to model, but

this is where I pulled out my last resort - the "masu necklace" - for its debut!

When I asked her to wear the necklace

and let me take photos, she said OK.

I did it!

Then, after my afternoon classes,

I had the teacher come to the rooftop

and she modeled for me!

On top of that,

I also asked her to be my English tutor during the period I'll be back in Japan.

*Since she's leaving the school, she'll have more free time at home, so she said OK.

Of course, since it's English study,

I'll be paying her.

Still, if I don't do anything during May and June after 3 months of English training, my skills will decline, so I'm glad I could make this negotiation.

Plus, she's the person I most want to talk to!

I did it!! (second time)

Also, on a different note,

that evening I invited some other teachers out to dinner.

Usually I'm the one being invited,

but this time, I was the one doing the inviting.

Involving teachers gives me more chances to speak English

than just talking with students.

I've hand-picked the teachers I enjoy talking to,

and if I keep inviting them out,

I feel like my level will keep going up. Haha.

With only 1 month left,

I think I'll be even more bold about this kind of stuff!

So all in all,

if you make a move, some kind of result will come!

That's the takeaway.

I only found out about the teacher's departure yesterday,

but I think I played the best hand I could.

I got the masu model shoot done,

secured a tutor for when I'm back in Japan,

and we even agreed to meet up in April.

*She'll have already left the school by then, so meeting outside one-on-one is no problem.

*I don't have romantic feelings, by the way.

I really believe that if there's something you want,

you should just keep taking action.

To build my English skills,

I'll do everything I can.

The old me couldn't push this hard,

but before I knew it, I'd become able to.

After dinner with the teachers that evening,

I headed to my usual bar for drinks.

Some Taiwanese and Korean friends joined later,

and we had a great time socializing.

excerpt_en: Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about the theme "Off to Zamboanga!" ▼Going where others don't So, starting today, there are no classes for 4 days. The reason is a Philippine religious holiday, and
body_en:
Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about the theme

"Off to Zamboanga!"

▼Going where others don't

So, starting today, there are no classes for 4 days.

The reason is a Philippine religious holiday,

and many shops will be closed.

School is no exception, so it's a break.

Since we students were given a 4-day holiday,

I headed to "Zamboanga" - a place nobody else would pick.

I bet very few people have even heard of it.

I like going to places where others don't.

So here's what my schedule looked like:

I woke up at 3 AM,

called a Grab, and caught a 5:20 AM flight.

I arrived in Zamboanga by 6:30 AM,

but with nothing particular to do and lots of luggage,

I headed straight to the hotel.

Grab wasn't available there,

so I had no choice but to ask a guy hanging around near the airport.

Since there was no meter running,

I figured during the ride that I'd get ripped off,

and sure enough, he quoted a ridiculously high price.

That said,

I wasn't about to just hand it over.

By telling him "That's too expensive!"

I managed to get the price down a bit.

When I arrived at the hotel,

I was so sleep-deprived and hungover from the night before

that I just wanted to lie down,

so I paid an extra 200 pesos for early check-in

and got the room set up.

Then I took a quick nap.

For lunch, I went out

to a nearby seafood restaurant.

Maybe because Japanese people are rare here,

I didn't see a single one.

Actually, there weren't any tourists at all.

*Super local.

I also checked out 2 cafes.

The afternoon got busy with work,

so I took care of that,

and then went out looking for dinner.

I found a shrimp restaurant.

Today was a religious holiday after all,

so less than half the shops were open.

But this place was open,

so I enjoyed some delicious shrimp and rice.

After that, I took a stroll.

I found a Catholic church.

Many people had gathered

and were chanting.

Japan doesn't have religion in the same way,

but having a system where "people gather for something" means that these gatherings also become "places to meet people."

The essence of religion, I think,

is connecting people and sharing memories.

*The nuance might be slightly different.

Near the church,

there were also many food stalls lined up.

I'd love to learn more about how the business works around here.

Also, not having seen a single Japanese person

made this quite a rare experience.

Zero Japanese people is pretty interesting.

Though I don't feel as nervous as I used to...

Anyway,

I could talk about so much more,

but I'm being hit by sleepiness

and writing any more is tough.

So instead,

I talked about Zamboanga on YouTube.

Check it out!

https://youtu.be/tMt3Xb3X15M?si=lSrcH6ZKXblDzjDm

excerpt_en: Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about the theme "Sightseeing and Learning." ▼Off to Santa Cruz Day 2 in Zamboanga. Today I headed from Zamboanga to Santa Cruz Island, which is about a 15-minute boat ride away.
body_en:
Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about the theme

"Sightseeing and Learning."

▼Off to Santa Cruz

Day 2 in Zamboanga.

Today, from the morning, I headed to Santa Cruz Island, which is about a 15-minute boat ride from Zamboanga.

The ocean there is supposedly gorgeous,

so I went to shoot photos of FOMUS's masu character "Masu-Game" as well.

From my accommodation to the ferry dock,

it was about a 10-minute tricycle ride.

In Cebu, I had been using Grab and taxis,

but Grab doesn't work at all in Zamboanga,

so it was my first time riding a tricycle.

I didn't know how to ride one

or how much it cost,

but the driver said, "You can pay whatever you want,"

so I paid 25 pesos,

but I have no idea if that was high or low. Haha.

Then I got to the ferry dock.

Santa Cruz Island costs 1,030 pesos to enter.

Also, there's a rule that you can only stay on the island from the morning until 2 PM. Since I was alone and the only thing I planned to do there was shoot photos, I chose the 2-hour stay course.

I had the boat all to myself! Haha.

Once I arrived, I shot Masu-Game photos,

took some videos,

and did some work on my phone.

It was ridiculously hot,

so I had pre-booked a cottage rental (100 pesos),

which was worth every peso.

Check out the Santa Cruz Island content here:

https://youtu.be/9cEkQ9kv6EU?si=Ti5kt4eHR3eMwY1O

So anyway,

I managed to get some really great photos

at Santa Cruz.

▼Getting ripped off because I'm a tourist

After coming back from the island,

I went looking for lunch.

After wandering around for about 20 minutes,

I found a Jollibee, so I went in.

Then,

I wanted to do a bit more sightseeing,

so I flagged down a tricycle to head to a tourist spot, but at this point I hadn't looked up the tricycle rates.

Normally you're supposed to ask the price before getting on,

but I got on without asking, so I was at the driver's mercy.

Plus, the tourist spot I went to

was closed for the day! Haha.

So I got dropped off and ended up doing nothing. Haha.

The tricycle ride itself was fun,

so it ended up being more of an activity than a taxi ride.

And since the price was at the driver's discretion,

I got charged more than double the going rate.

Doing your research beforehand

really is important for everything.

That was a good lesson learned.

Then in the evening, I went wandering around looking for a restaurant again.

Yesterday and today are Holy Week,

and for religious reasons, supermarkets and such are closed.

The only food place that seemed to be open

was the shrimp restaurant I went to yesterday.

But because of that, it was packed,

so I gave up on the shrimp place.

I went to a restaurant nearby instead.

They had beef curry on the menu,

so I ordered it,

but the portions were tiny! Haha.

And it still cost about 800 yen in Japanese currency.

I wasn't exactly ripped off,

but it felt like a pretty pricey meal.

Also,

because of the religious holiday,

lots of people were walking around at night.

Almost like a parade.

Getting to see this kind of scenery was nice.

These 2 days have been great for getting to know the city of Zamboanga.

Starting tomorrow, I have several Masu Photo shoots lined up.

Going to enjoy it!

excerpt_en: Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about the theme "Masu Photo in Zamboanga!" ▼The fruits of English study Day 3 of activities in Zamboanga. Today I spent the entire day doing Masu Photo shoots.
body_en:
Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about the theme

"Masu Photo in Zamboanga!"

▼The fruits of English study

Day 3 of activities in Zamboanga.

Today I spent the entire day doing Masu Photo shoots.

Once again, I recruited people in Zamboanga

who were available for shoots through FOMUS's Instagram account.

Of those, I shot with 3 people today.

I had been chatting with them

since I was still in Cebu,

but back then I knew nothing about tourist spots in Zamboanga,

so I basically let the models choose the shooting locations.

The first person

was someone who works as an office worker at a university in Cebu.

Naturally,

it was her first time seeing the traditional Japanese craft "masu" in person.

However,

she said she loves Japanese anime, culture, and crafts,

and the fact that a Japanese photographer

was coming to where she lives

is what made her decide to participate.

We did the shoot at a cafe,

and the actual shooting took about 10-15 minutes.

After that, we just talked the whole time.

Of course, she doesn't speak Japanese.

Everything was in English,

and maybe it's the payoff from 2 months of English immersion in Cebu,

but I felt zero stress.

Also, when I explained about FOMUS

using my iPad and phone with images,

it was in English too,

and it was worlds easier compared to

my conversations in Kuala Lumpur last year.

Even I was surprised by the improvement! Haha.

The second person

was a professional model active in Zamboanga.

The shoot focused on wearing FOMUS's accessory brand "FOMUSParure."

This shoot also wrapped up in about 10-20 minutes.

I wanted to spend the time on conversation,

because I wanted to hear what they had to say

and I wanted them to learn about FOMUS and me.

Honestly, when it comes to shooting,

I've done an insane number of shoots,

so I can get decent photos no matter where I am.

Especially since it's Masu Photo. Haha.

While chatting with the model,

she was absolutely loving the masu accessories.

The merit of having created

FOMUS Parure (the accessory brand)

isn't just the high profit margins -

it's that professional models

find it really fascinating.

There are handmade accessories

all over the world,

but a brand specializing in the traditional Japanese craft "masu"

is only us.

For models who want to wear something different from everyone else,

it's an incredibly appealing item.

Doing things differently from others really is important!

And then, the third person.

We moved from the cafe to Paseo del Mar,

a popular tourist spot.

Of course, the third model was the one who told me about the place. Haha.

It was already 2 PM,

and it was just insanely hot outside!!

We did the shoot in about 20 minutes.

Since this was my last shoot for the day,

and the model said she had nothing else planned afterward,

she took me on a tour and we chatted at a cafe.

We used a tricycle (motorcycle transport) to get around,

and when I'm alone, I get charged tourist prices,

but having a local with me made all the difference!

No one tried to overcharge us! Haha.

Thanks to that, we were able to travel at regular prices.

Having friends and acquaintances

in each place you visit really is great!

Once we got to the cafe,

we talked about all sorts of things.

In English, of course.

This person is still a university student

who wants to become a cabin attendant after graduation.

I always want to root for young people taking on challenges,

and in 3 years (after graduation),

she'll apparently be coming to Japan more often for work.

Even if she doesn't go the CA route,

meeting someone from Zamboanga with an exciting future ahead

is a big deal.

▼Valuing connections

With all 3 people today,

I made sure to carve out plenty of time for conversation after the shoot,

and it really is incredibly important.

I was able to clearly convey why I'm traveling the world taking photos with FOMUS masu,

and the models could understand that participating in Masu Photo brings them significant benefits too.

From the model's perspective,

they don't get paid for doing a Masu Photo shoot.

Only those who understand that

can participate in Masu Photo.

That's why

it naturally filters in the right people.

Furthermore, having continued Masu Photo shoots

over the past 3 years in Japan, Taiwan, and Malaysia

is an incredibly strong asset.

I can tell because I show them

the Masu Photo collection before the shoot.

"I'm going to be in the Philippines edition of this!"

They can understand that

with just one look.

On top of that,

models who've taken similar Masu Photos

exist all around the world, creating a shared bond.

And I connect people through FOMUS.

In other words, getting involved with FOMUS

means making friends and allies all over the world.

This is something AI absolutely cannot replicate.

Only humans can do this.

And it's not just me -

anyone who has a FOMUS masu can freely

go out for drinks with it, turn it into art,

and proactively use it however they want,

so this benefit is available to anyone.

The fact that I could convey all this

to models in Zamboanga

and they found it really fascinating - that's everything.

For me too,

it means more FOMUS fans,

and I get practical English conversation practice,

so the benefits are huge!

Actually going there in person and connecting

is powerful in any country.

Also, purely and simply,

I just love wandering around foreign countries on my own

which is also a big factor.

If I were the type who

can't go anywhere without friends,

my actions would be limited,

so it's been good that I've been strengthening my ability to move around on my own in various places.

So that's the story of today -

Masu Photo shoots in Zamboanga

and more new friends and allies.

I'll keep doing

what only FOMUS and I can do!!

*First time getting the local price on a tricycle!

excerpt_en: Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about the theme "Creating Memories." ▼Last day in Zamboanga So, today is the last day of activities in Zamboanga! And I had 2 shoots today as well.
body_en:
Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about the theme

"Creating Memories."

▼Last day in Zamboanga

So, today is the last day of activities in Zamboanga!

And today,

I had 2 shoots.

The first one was at a cafe

about a 3-minute walk from my hotel.

She arrived about 60 minutes

after the meeting time,

but I don't get upset about that anymore.

Because I understand that it's normal for people in the Philippines to be late.

Even though I understand that, I still show up on time because there's always that thought of "what if they actually come on time." Haha.

That said, since it was a cafe meetup,

I just did my own work while waiting.

*There's always plenty to do.

When the model arrived, it was time for the Masu Photo shoot!

We shot inside and outside the cafe.

The masu accessories looked great on her!

Then, on to the second shoot.

I had left the location entirely up to the model,

so I headed to the place she specified.

I've gotten the hang of tricycles (the local transport) by now.

I learned how to negotiate properly

so I don't get charged tourist prices anymore.

This time,

the location had a view of the ocean.

I had requested "a place where you can see the ocean," and her mother looked it up for us.

Thank you, Mom!

I got some solid photos here as well.

Once again, being able to do Masu Photo shoots

in a place like Zamboanga is something to be very happy about.

For me too,

it's great to see the FOMUS brand expanding.

After the shoot, I had a little time before my flight, so I rushed into a nearby restaurant.

*3 PM.

*The shoot overlapped with lunch, so I hadn't been able to eat.

I ordered a noodle dish.

It was incredibly delicious.

▼Shared memories are powerful

Now, what I've come to realize once again is

the importance of creating memories.

You might think it's all about having the right items,

but it's actually not - collaborative experiences with someone are ultimately the strongest.

If it were just taking photos, it wouldn't create lasting memories (since you can do that anytime), but our Masu Photo is a project you literally can't do without having a masu.

Plus, having the track record of shooting in Japan, Taiwan, and Malaysia adds to the appeal!

Also, after the shoots,

I make time to increase our conversation.

By talking about why FOMUS exists

and why now is the time to do this,

I can communicate where FOMUS and I are headed.

With AI advancing so rapidly lately,

I'm willing to invest money and time

in creating memories that AI cannot.

I think this kind of thing

will only become more valuable going forward.

But most people

won't realize that for a while yet.

Probably 5+ years.

▼Zamboanga wrap-up

And with that,

my four days of activities in Zamboanga have come to an end.

I was surprised that there were zero tourists,

but precisely because of that, being able to immerse myself in a thoroughly local environment was a great experience.

*In Cebu, there are tons of tourists and exchange students.

And what I noticed is that

I'm thoroughly enjoying

this kind of environment.

Apparently, even people from Cebu

find going to Zamboanga alone a bit scary,

but personally, I see value in this experience,

and I made new allies in a completely unknown land

who find FOMUS's activities interesting.

In terms of pure sightseeing,

it might be a place that leaves you wanting more,

but when you bring FOMUS along,

you can have a great time anywhere.

That was a huge takeaway.

I'll be going to other countries from here,

and having grasped this feeling is nothing but an asset for me.

For the models I met too,

they were surprised that a Japanese photographer

chose Zamboanga of all places to visit,

and then he's carrying around a masu on top of that. Haha.

They really seemed to enjoy themselves.

Also, I edit and send all the photos the very next day, which they appreciated as well.

Once again,

I feel that FOMUS masu holds

the potential to connect the world.

When I get back to Japan,

at some point,

it might be fun to hold

a FOMUS global meeting!

So yeah,

it was an incredibly fulfilling 4 days in Zamboanga.

I'd love to go back if I get the chance!

https://www.notion.so

excerpt_en: Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about the theme "One Month Left!" ▼Clear results So, my activities in Cebu have entered their 9th week. My graduation date is April 26th, so there's 1 month left.
body_en:
Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about the theme

"One Month Left!"

▼Clear results

So, my activities in Cebu have entered their 9th week.

My graduation date is April 26th, so there's 1 month left.

*4 more weeks.

I can't believe 2 months have already passed...

Time really flies.

So anyway,

the scores from the test I took last Wednesday are in.

Here are the results:

And here were the previous results (1st month):

Test scores aren't everything,

but since I had been doing talk-centered classes without textbooks, my speaking skills have been steadily improving.

However, my reading score went down,

and writing dropped too. Haha.

For listening, I apparently have trouble picking up machine-generated speech, so my score plateaued there.

Score-wise,

the results were somewhat underwhelming,

but in terms of actual conversation and communication,

I've built real confidence over these 2 months.

*Not that I can speak perfectly, though.

I really feel that

devoting concentrated time to something is crucial.

That said, with only 1 month left,

there's a limit to what I can do.

In my case,

if I can just get my speaking and listening down,

I can manage working overseas for now,

so I want to kick it into an even higher gear from here.

▼Shoot schedules are coming together

As originally planned,

I have shoots with professional models in Cebu lined up for April.

Back in February,

I had sent out a ton of emails

and hardly got any replies, which had me worried,

but after changing my email approach, I started getting much better responses.

As a result, I've confirmed shoots with multiple models.

The shoot location will be a luxury hotel's beach and pool.

I personally wanted to see Cebu's high-end luxury hotels, and since I'm going anyway, I wanted to make it work,

so I'll be doing shoots with participating professional models

at a private beach.

For the professional models only,

I'm providing a talent fee, beach admission, and lunch.

Plus,

the right to be a FOMUS Parure ambassador.

Honestly,

it's costing a lot of money.

But that's how badly I want

to take the FOMUS brand global.

That's why I need the models to shine.

With this being the final month,

I'm fired up about a lot of things.

25 days left of activities in Cebu.

I'll give it everything I've got with no regrets!!
